items.title: Conflict Resolution Wizard
items.description: When a sync detects that a target harness has been manually modified since last sync, surface a diff-based conflict resolution flow instead of silently overwriting. Users who maintain multiple harnesses semi-independently lose customizations today; this gives them control over what wins. Like git merge but for AI harness configs.

items.title: Per-Harness Config Overrides
items.description: Let users annotate CLAUDE.md sections with `<!-- harness:codex-only -->` or `<!-- skip:gemini -->` metadata so certain rules stay harness-specific. Power users often have Codex-specific tool permissions or Gemini-specific context that shouldn't bleed everywhere. Avoids the current 'all-or-nothing' sync model.

items.title: Team Config Profiles via Git
items.description: Introduce a `.harnessync/profiles/` convention that stores named config snapshots in the repo, allowing teams to share a canonical harness config via git. Solves the 'everyone on my team uses different AI tool settings' problem by making harness config a first-class team artifact.

items.title: /sync-onboard: Interactive Setup Wizard
items.description: A guided first-run command that detects installed harnesses, asks what to sync where, sets up accounts, and creates an initial snapshot — all interactively. Today new users must read docs and hand-edit config; this removes the cold-start friction and gets users to first successful sync in under 2 minutes.

items.title: Capability Gap Report
items.description: Generate a report showing exactly which CC features have no equivalent in each target harness, ranked by how much value the user is 'losing'. E.g. 'Your 12 custom agents have no equivalent in Codex — here's the best approximation'. Helps users make informed decisions about which harness to use for which task.

items.title: Auto-Detect Newly Installed Harnesses
items.description: Watch for new harness installations (Cursor, Aider, Gemini CLI, etc.) and proactively offer to configure them with the user's existing CC config. Users who try a new AI tool want it working in minutes, not after manually running setup. Eliminates the 'I installed X and have to set it up from scratch' frustration.

items.title: Named Sync Profiles (Work/Personal/OSS)
items.description: Let users define multiple named profiles (e.g. `work`, `personal`, `client-acme`) with different source paths and target sets, switchable via `/sync --profile work`. Developers working on multiple clients or contexts have fundamentally different configs; today they must manually swap files.

items.title: MCP Server Discovery & Cross-Harness Registration
items.description: Scan for running or configured MCP servers across the user's machine and automatically register them in all harnesses that support MCP. Users spend hours manually adding the same MCP server to CC, Cursor, and Gemini separately. Make MCP server setup a one-time action.

items.title: Config Health Score & Recommendations
items.description: Score the user's CC config on portability, completeness, and best practices (0-100), with actionable recommendations like 'Add a project description to improve Codex translation quality by ~30%'. Gives users a clear target to optimize toward rather than guessing what makes configs sync well.

items.title: Harness Benchmark: Compare Output Quality
items.description: Run a standard prompt set across all configured harnesses and display side-by-side response quality, so users can see which harness performs best with their current config. Helps users decide which harness to use for which task type, informed by their actual setup rather than marketing claims.

items.title: AI-Assisted Rule Translation for Unmappable Settings
items.description: When a CC feature has no direct equivalent in a target harness, use Claude to generate a best-effort approximation (e.g. translate a CC hook into a Cursor rule that approximates the behavior). Today unmappable settings are silently skipped; this makes the gap visible and fills it intelligently.

items.title: Auto-Generated Sync Changelog
items.description: After each sync, generate a human-readable changelog entry describing what changed and why (e.g. 'Added 3 new MCP servers to Codex, updated Gemini rules from CLAUDE.md section 2'). Developers lose track of when configs diverged; this creates an audit trail without any manual effort.

items.title: Dry-Run Preview with Diff Output
items.description: Show exactly what files would be created, modified, or deleted before any sync actually runs, formatted as a readable diff per harness. Users are afraid to sync because they don't know what will change; dry-run builds confidence and prevents surprise overwrites.

items.title: CI/CD Config Export for Headless Environments
items.description: Export harness configs in a format suitable for CI/CD pipelines (GitHub Actions, GitLab CI) where AI coding tools run headlessly. Teams that use AI tools in CI face a completely manual config problem today; this makes their local CC setup portable to automated environments.

items.title: Plugin Compatibility Matrix
items.description: For each installed CC plugin, show which of its features (hooks, skills, agents, commands) have equivalents in each target harness, and what the translation fidelity is. Plugin authors and power users need to know the blast radius of a plugin sync before committing to it.

items.title: Starter Config Templates by Role/Stack
items.description: Provide curated CLAUDE.md + harness config templates for common developer roles (Python backend, React frontend, DevOps, ML engineer) that users can adopt as a starting point. Most users have weak configs because starting from blank is hard; templates provide immediate value and sync-ready structure.

items.title: Section-Level Sync Selection UI
items.description: Interactive `/sync-select` command that lets users check/uncheck CLAUDE.md sections, individual skills, or specific MCP servers before syncing. Power users want surgical control — sync the coding style rules but not the company-internal tool config. Today it's all-or-nothing.

items.title: Sync Notifications (Desktop / Slack / ntfy)
items.description: Send a notification when an auto-sync completes, fails, or detects drift, via macOS notifications, Slack webhook, or ntfy. Users running watch-mode syncs in the background have no visibility into what happened; silent success and silent failure are equally invisible today.

items.title: Project-Scoped Sync Rules (.harnessync)
items.description: Support a per-project `.harnessync` config file that overrides global sync behavior — e.g. 'never sync MCP servers for this repo' or 'always sync to Cursor only'. Different projects have different sensitivity (OSS vs internal); per-project control prevents accidental leakage of internal configs.

items.title: Natural Language Config Authoring
items.description: A `/sync-author` command where users describe rules in plain English and HarnessSync generates the appropriate CLAUDE.md sections formatted for maximum cross-harness portability. Most users write CLAUDE.md poorly, which degrades translation quality; guided authoring produces better source configs.

items.title: Harness Migration Assistant
items.description: Step-by-step guided flow to help users migrate their primary harness from Codex/Gemini/Cursor to Claude Code, importing their existing config. Users who want to standardize on CC face a painful manual migration; this makes CC adoption zero-friction.

items.title: MCP Server Cross-Harness Compatibility Check
items.description: Before syncing an MCP server config to a target harness, verify the server is actually reachable and the target harness supports that transport type (stdio vs SSE vs HTTP). Users silently get broken MCP configs in target harnesses today because compatibility is never validated.

items.title: Team Config Server (Local HTTP)
items.description: Spin up a local HTTP server that exposes the canonical CC config as a read-only API, so all team members' harness tools can pull from a single team source. Teams with shared workstations or pair-programming contexts want one machine to be the config authority without using git.

items.title: Config Version Pinning & Time Travel
items.description: Let users pin a target harness to a specific config snapshot and only update it on explicit promotion. Users in the middle of a complex debugging session don't want their Codex config to change mid-session because they edited CLAUDE.md. Stable environments matter.

items.title: Skill Coverage Heatmap
items.description: Visual report showing which of your CC skills have high/medium/low fidelity translations in each target harness, colored by quality. Users invest heavily in building CC skills and have no visibility into which ones 'work' outside CC. This makes the invisible visible.

items.title: Auto-Detect Harness Version Updates
items.description: Monitor when target harnesses (Codex, Gemini CLI, Cursor) update to a new version and re-evaluate whether previously-unmappable features can now be synced. Harnesses gain new capabilities regularly; today HarnessSync adapters must be manually updated to take advantage of them.

items.title: Sync Impact Preview: 'What Will Break'
items.description: Before syncing, analyze which in-progress conversations or open files in target harnesses would be affected by the config change and warn the user. Syncing while a Cursor session is active can break mid-conversation behavior; proactive warnings prevent frustrating interruptions.

items.title: Secret & Sensitive Value Masking
items.description: Detect and mask secrets, API keys, or internal URLs in MCP server configs or rules before syncing to shared or less-trusted harnesses. Users accidentally sync internal endpoint URLs or auth tokens to Cursor configs that get committed to git; automatic masking prevents this class of leak.

items.title: Auto-Generate Config Documentation
items.description: Generate a human-readable summary of what your CC config does — rules, skills, agents — formatted as a README-style doc that non-technical teammates can understand. Teams onboarding new developers struggle to explain 'what our AI config does'; auto-docs make the invisible setup legible.

items.title: Cursor Rules Bidirectional Sync
items.description: Full bidirectional sync with Cursor's `.cursor/rules/` directory, including reading Cursor-specific rule types (auto-attached, agent-requested, always-active) and mapping them to CC equivalents. Cursor is now the most popular AI IDE; treating it as a first-class peer (not just a target) unlocks a huge user segment.

items.title: GitHub Copilot Workspace Adapter
items.description: Add GitHub Copilot as a sync target, writing rules to `.github/copilot-instructions.md` and mapping MCP servers to Copilot's extension config. Copilot has the largest installed base of any AI coding tool; excluding it leaves the most popular harness unsupported.

items.title: Zed AI Adapter
items.description: Add Zed's AI assistant as a sync target, mapping CC rules to Zed's context server and assistant config format. Zed is growing fast among performance-focused developers and has its own AI config format; early support captures that audience before they build manual workflows.

items.title: Config Complexity Analyzer & Simplifier
items.description: Scan CLAUDE.md and skills for redundant rules, conflicting instructions, or sections that cancel each other out, and suggest a simplified version. Complex CC configs degrade sync quality because ambiguous rules translate poorly; cleaner source means better cross-harness output.

items.title: Sync Watchdog: Persistent Background Daemon
items.description: A lightweight background process (launchd on macOS, systemd on Linux) that watches for CC config changes and syncs in real time without requiring CC to be running. Today watch mode requires an active CC session; a daemon makes sync continuous and invisible, the way Dropbox works.

items.title: Extensible Sync Lint Rules
items.description: Let users and teams define custom lint rules that run before every sync (e.g. 'rules must not reference internal hostnames', 'all skills must have a description'). Teams have internal compliance requirements that generic tools can't anticipate; extensible lint puts enforcement in user hands.

items.title: Multi-Machine Config Sync via iCloud/Dotfiles Repo
items.description: Integrate with iCloud Drive or a user-specified dotfiles git repo to keep CC config — and therefore all downstream harness configs — in sync across the user's multiple machines. Developers work on laptop and desktop; config drift between machines is a daily nuisance that this eliminates.

items.title: Interactive Conflict Resolver
items.description: When a target harness has local modifications that diverge from Claude Code's source of truth, surface a diff-based UI in the terminal letting users choose per-section: keep local, accept source, or merge. Currently drift is silently overwritten, which destroys intentional per-harness customizations users made directly in Codex or OpenCode.

items.title: Dry-Run Preview Mode
items.description: Add a `/sync --dry-run` flag that shows exactly what would change across all targets without writing anything. Displays a color-coded diff per target file. Users are afraid to run sync on production setups without knowing what will change — this removes that anxiety and builds trust in the tool.

items.title: Harness Health Dashboard
items.description: A `/sync-health` command that checks whether each target harness is installed, authenticated, and functional — not just whether config files exist. Shows version info, detected capabilities, and known compatibility issues. Users waste time debugging sync failures that are actually 'Gemini CLI not installed' situations.

items.title: Selective Sync Rules
items.description: Let users annotate CLAUDE.md sections with tags like `<!-- sync:exclude -->` or `<!-- sync:codex-only -->` to control what gets synced where. Some rules are Claude Code-specific (referencing MCP tools that don't exist in Codex) and blindly syncing them pollutes other harnesses with unusable instructions.

items.title: Reverse Sync Import
items.description: A `/sync-import` command that reads config from a target harness (Codex AGENTS.md, OpenCode skills) and merges useful content back into Claude Code. Users who started on Codex before discovering Claude Code have existing institutional knowledge trapped in AGENTS.md that they can't easily migrate.

items.title: Sync Profiles
items.description: Named sync profiles (e.g., 'work', 'oss', 'client-x') that control which accounts and targets are active per project. Switch with `/sync --profile work`. Users who consult for multiple clients need completely different sync targets per project and currently have to manually reconfigure every time.

items.title: MCP Capability Compatibility Matrix
items.description: When syncing MCP servers, automatically detect which servers have native equivalents or partial support in each target harness, and surface a compatibility report. Users don't know which of their 20 MCP servers will actually work in Gemini CLI vs. silently fail — this removes the guesswork.

items.title: Auto-Sync on CLAUDE.md Save
items.description: Extend the existing PostToolUse hook to also trigger sync when Claude Code writes CLAUDE.md or settings.json via file-watching (fswatch/inotify). The current hook only fires on Claude Code tool use, missing the case where users edit config files directly in their editor.

items.title: Skill Coverage Gap Detector
items.description: Analyze which Claude Code skills have no equivalent in target harnesses and surface these as 'coverage gaps' with suggestions for how to approximate them. Users assume their Claude Code workflows are replicated everywhere but discover gaps only when they switch harnesses mid-task.

items.title: Sync Changelog Feed
items.description: Maintain a human-readable `.harness-sync/changelog.md` that logs every sync event with timestamps, what changed, and which targets were updated. Users in teams need an audit trail of when config changed and why — currently sync is a black box with no history.

items.title: Team Config Sharing via Git
items.description: A `/sync-export` command that generates a portable, shareable `.harness-sync/team-config.json` that teammates can import to replicate your multi-account setup. Onboarding new team members to the same harness configuration currently requires manual documentation and tribal knowledge.

items.title: Cross-Harness Prompt Benchmarker
items.description: Run a canned prompt against multiple harnesses simultaneously and show response quality, latency, and cost side-by-side. Helps users decide which harness to use for which task category. Users have no data-driven way to evaluate whether their Claude Code investment actually performs better than Gemini or Codex for specific workflows.

items.title: Permission Model Advisor
items.description: Detect when Claude Code's allow/deny tool permissions have no equivalent in a target harness and proactively warn with specific advice (e.g., 'Codex has no bash restriction equivalent — consider adding a guard comment in AGENTS.md'). Currently permission drift is invisible and creates security inconsistencies.

items.title: Harness Usage Analytics
items.description: Track locally (no telemetry) which harnesses are actually being used and how often, then surface this in `/sync-status` to help users decide which targets to keep syncing. Users often have 4 harnesses configured but only use 2 — the dead weight creates noise and slows sync.

items.title: Conditional Sync Triggers
items.description: Let users configure sync to trigger only under specific conditions: only on git branch changes, only when certain files change, or only at specific times. Power users want surgical control over when expensive sync operations fire rather than syncing on every Claude Code file write.

items.title: Skill Translation Engine
items.description: Intelligently translate Claude Code skill markdown into target-native formats, stripping Claude Code-specific tool calls and rewriting them as generic instructions. Currently skills are copied verbatim, leaving Codex with references to Bash/Edit/Write tools it doesn't understand.

items.title: New Harness Auto-Detector
items.description: Periodically scan PATH and common install locations for newly installed AI coding CLIs and prompt the user to add them as sync targets. Users install Windsurf or Cursor-CLI and forget to add it to HarnessSync — they only notice weeks later when it's hopelessly out of date.

items.title: Config Snapshot & Restore
items.description: Before every sync, snapshot the current state of all target configs so users can roll back with `/sync-restore --date 2026-03-10`. Users who push a bad CLAUDE.md change have no way to recover target harness configs that were overwritten.

items.title: Environment Variable Propagation
items.description: Sync relevant environment variables (API keys, tool paths, custom endpoints) across harness config files where the target format supports it, with warnings when a variable has no target equivalent. Users repeatedly re-enter the same API keys in every harness's settings.

items.title: Pre-Commit Sync Gate
items.description: A git pre-commit hook installer that runs sync-status before commits and fails if any target is out of sync, preventing commits with stale harness configs. Teams who review each other's CLAUDE.md changes need assurance that Codex and Gemini are always current.

items.title: Per-Harness Override Files
items.description: Support `.harness-sync/overrides/codex.md` files that get appended to the synced AGENTS.md for Codex-specific instructions. Users need to add harness-specific context without polluting CLAUDE.md — currently they're forced to either duplicate or go without.

items.title: AI-Powered Rule Optimizer
items.description: Use Claude to analyze CLAUDE.md and suggest which rules are redundant, conflicting, or too verbose for target harnesses, then offer to auto-optimize. Rules accumulate over time and many become outdated or contradictory — users have no systematic way to audit them.

items.title: Sync Notification Webhooks
items.description: After sync, POST a summary payload to a configurable webhook URL (Slack, Discord, custom endpoint). Teams using shared Claude Code configs in a repo need visibility when someone updates the rules that affect everyone's harness.

items.title: Feature Parity Report
items.description: A `/sync-parity` command that produces a structured report of every Claude Code feature in use (MCP servers, skills, rules, agents) with a per-target compatibility score and specific gaps. Gives users a clear picture of what they're giving up when working in a non-Claude harness.

items.title: Project-Aware Sync Scope
items.description: Detect the current git repo and automatically scope sync to project-level configs only when inside a project, falling back to global when at home. Users constantly forget to specify scope and accidentally sync global rules when they meant project-only.

items.title: Pre-Sync Config Linter
items.description: Before syncing, validate that CLAUDE.md and settings.json are well-formed, check for common mistakes (broken skill references, invalid JSON, missing required fields), and refuse to sync invalid configs with actionable error messages. Bad configs silently propagate to all targets and are hard to debug.

items.title: Terminal Status Bar Badge
items.description: A lightweight daemon that displays sync drift status in shell prompt or tmux status bar (e.g., `[HS: 2 stale]`). Users only discover drift when things break — ambient visibility means they address it proactively.

items.title: Bulk Account Onboarding Wizard
items.description: An interactive wizard that discovers all installed harnesses, detects existing account configurations, and sets up multi-account sync in one guided session instead of per-harness manual configuration. The current setup process requires separate steps per harness and account, discouraging full adoption.

items.title: MCP Server Registry Lookup
items.description: When syncing MCP servers, automatically check a community registry for target-native alternatives (e.g., 'The GitHub MCP you use has a Gemini-native extension — want to use that instead?'). Users get better harness-native behavior rather than generic wrapped servers.

items.title: Scheduled Sync with Cron Integration
items.description: Allow users to schedule automatic syncs at off-peak times using system cron or HarnessSync's built-in scheduler. Users who make many rapid config changes don't want sync to fire on every change but do want eventual consistency without manual intervention.

items.title: Cross-Harness Secret Sanitizer
items.description: Scan source configs for patterns matching secrets (API keys, tokens, passwords) before syncing and either redact them or warn the user. Users accidentally commit API keys into CLAUDE.md which then get propagated to AGENTS.md that may be checked into version control.

items.title: Harness Adoption Coach
items.description: After sync, surface a tip about a Claude Code feature the user has configured that they haven't enabled in other harnesses yet, with a one-click setup. Users don't know what they're missing across harnesses — proactive coaching drives deeper adoption.

items.title: Config Evolution Graph
items.description: Track how CLAUDE.md and target configs have evolved over time and visualize the history in a terminal-friendly graph with `/sync-history`. Users working on long-lived projects want to understand when and why their config grew, and which rules have been stable vs. volatile.

items.title: Sync Self-Test Suite
items.description: A `/sync-test` command that creates isolated temp directories, runs a full sync cycle with fixture configs, and verifies correctness without touching real harness configs. Power users and contributors need a safe way to validate sync behavior after changes.

items.title: Community Rules Hub
items.description: A curated directory of shareable CLAUDE.md rule packs (e.g., 'TypeScript Best Practices', 'Python TDD Pack') that users can pull directly into their config with `/sync-install typescript-rules`. Users spend hours writing rules from scratch that the community has already solved.

items.title: Consider consolidating sync-* commands (5 commands)
items.description: There are 5 commands with prefix "sync-": sync-health.md, sync-parity.md, sync-restore.md, sync-setup.md, sync-status.md. Consider whether some could be subcommands of a single parent command for a cleaner UX.
